tate of CaliforI1ia 
Fair Political Practices CommissioEf. 
P.o. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Mr. Walter I. Colby 
County Counsel 
County of Yuba 
Courthouse 
Marysville, California 
95901 
Dear Mr. Colby: 
September 21, 1976 
This letter is in response to your letter to the Com-
mission of September 3, 1976, requesting advice pursuant to 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18329. 
It also is intended to acknowledge 
the substance of a telephone conversation you and I had on September 
9, 1976. 
The facts, as we understand them, are as follows: 
A mem-
ber of the Board of Supervisors owns land which will be directly 
affected by a proposed change in zoning of portions of the County. 
This change will, in essence, rezone certain agricultural land to 
permit limited residential development. 
It is anticipated that this 
change will materially increase the value of the land. 
The Super-
visor in question is one of approximately 20 persons holding interests 
in the affected area and he does not use the land as a residence. 
We think it is clear, and you apparently agree, that the 
Supervisor has a financial interest in the decision as defined in 
Government Code Section 87103, and that he, therefore, should not 
"make, participate in the making, or use his official position to 
influence" the deci sion. 
Government Code Section 87100. 
See, 
Opinion requested by Thomas W. Oglesby, 1 FPPC Opinions 71 (No. 
75-083, July 2, 1975). 
In other words, the Supervisor should dis-
qualify himself in the matter. 
There remains, however, the question of what the Super-
visor may do in order to protect his own interests in this matter. 
We think that the Commission's regulation, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Sections 
18700 (d) and (f) I is apposite. 
The regulation provides that "mak-
ing or participating in the making of a governmental decision" and 
"in any way attempting to use his or her official position to in-
fl uence a governmen tal deci sion" shall not inel ude : 
TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE/ADMINISTRATION (916) 322.5660 • 
EXECUTIVE/LEGAl

"",.I"\DrrHn'T 'r ....... r .. ~~ ~r ... ~r_rr_ --_ •.• 


Mr 
Wa1 ter 1. Colby 
Page Two 
September 21, 1976 
Appearances by a public official as a member of the 
general public before an agency in the course of its 
prescribed governmental function to represent himself 
or herself on matters related solely to his or her per-
sonal interests ... 
2 Cal. Adm. Code §18700 (d) and (f) 
This exception was designed to insure a proper balance be-
tween the seemingly competing interests of insuring unbiased official 
decision making and of protecting the rights of public officials whose 
interests are being affected by governmental decisions. 
Pursuant to 
the exception, it is permissible for the Supervisor in question to 
disqualify himself from voting or participating in the official dis-
cussions of the matter in question and to refrain from using his of-
ficial position to influence those discussions; to "step-down" into 
the audience; and to appear as a member of the public on his own be-
half. 
Of course, in so doing the appearance by the Supervisor must 
be constrained by the same procedures (time limits, etc.) that would 
condition the appearance of other members of the public. 
Further-
more, the regulation contemplates appearances by the public official 
related solely to his personal interests. 
The exception would not 
apply to any appearance by the official before his or her agency in 
a representative capacity. 
I also want to mention that the Commission is going to dis-
cuss the meaning of 2 Cal. Adm. Code Sections 18700 (d) and (f) dur-
ing its October meeting. 
If the Commission's conclusions are dif-
ferent than those expressed in this letter, I will contact you. 
How-
ever, pending any determination by the Commission that the advice here-
in is incorrect, the Supervisor is entitled to rely on the advice and 
will not be subject to any enforcement proceeding by the Fair Political 
Practices Commission as a result of such re 
ance. 
See 2 Cal. Adm. 
Code Section 18329 (e). 
I trust this advice resolves your question. 
If we can be 
of further assistance, please do not hesitate to call me. 
KWG:jo 
Sincerely, 
Kenneth W. Goshorn 
Research Specialist 


,state of California 
Memorandum 
To 
Ken Finney and participants in 
Opinion Request Meeting 
Date 
September 14, 197 
From 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
Ken Goshorn 
Subject: Walter Colby letter of September 3. 
On September 9, 1976, I spoke with Mr. Colby on the phone -
the 
following additional facts were developed. 
1. 
The zoning change in question is to rezone agricultural 
land on the outskirts of the City of Marysville so that 
5-acre residential lots may be developed. 
2. 
It is foreseeable that this change will be reflected 
in a material increase in value to the land in question. 
3. 
The land owned by the concerned official is within the 
area and is not just a residence. 
4. 
Only about 20 land owners will be directly affected by 
the zoning change. 
They own varying amounts of property. 
The official is one of these 20 or so people. 
5. 
Mr. Colby also wants answered the question of what the 
official may do under 18700(f) of the Regulations. 
KWG: jhm

sections.question: What may a Board of Supervisors member do to protect his own financial interests when he owns land that will be directly affected by a proposed zoning change, given that he must disqualify himself from the official decision?
sections.conclusion: It is permissible for the Supervisor in question to disqualify himself from voting or participating in the official discussions of the matter in question and to refrain from using his official position to influence those discussions; to 'step-down' into the audience; and to appear as a member of the public on his own behalf. Such appearance must be constrained by the same procedures (time limits, etc.) that would condition the appearance of other members of the public and must relate solely to his personal interests, not in any representative capacity.
sections.question_synthetic: A county supervisor owns land that will be materially affected by a proposed zoning change and must disqualify himself from voting on the decision. The question is what steps the supervisor may take to represent and protect his personal financial interests in the matter.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The supervisor may disqualify himself from the official decision-making process and then appear before the agency as a private citizen to represent his own personal financial interests, subject to the same procedural constraints as any other member of the public. This exception balances the need for unbiased official decision-making with the protection of public officials' rights when their interests are affected.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes an FPPC internal memorandum dated September 14, 1976 summarizing facts developed during a phone call, and an incoming request letter from County Counsel Walter Colby dated September 3, 1976. The main advice letter is dated September 21, 1976 and signed by Kenneth W. Goshorn. Clear Q/C structure present with explicit conclusion. Includes caveat that FPPC will discuss the regulation during October meeting and will contact if conclusions change.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What may a Board of Supervisors member do to protect his own financial interests when he owns land that will be directly affected by a proposed zoning change, given that he must disqualify himself from the official decision?

CONCLUSION: It is permissible for the Supervisor in question to disqualify himself from voting or participating in the official discussions of the matter in question and to refrain from using his official position to influence those discussions; to 'step-down' into the audience; and to appear as a member of the public on his own behalf. Such appearance must be constrained by the same procedures (time limits, etc.) that would condition the appearance of other members of the public and must relate solely to his personal interests, not in any representative capacity.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises that a County Supervisor with a financial interest in a zoning decision must disqualify himself from voting or participating in official discussions, but may then appear before the agency as a private citizen to represent his personal interests. The supervisor is entitled to rely on this advice and will not be subject to enforcement proceedings by the FPPC.
